flashcards.front: What is a Circular Queue and how does it differ from a linear queue?
flashcards.back: A Circular Queue is a linear data structure that connects the last array position to the first, forming a circle. Unlike linear queues, it reuses spaces vacated by dequeued elements instead of wasting them.

flashcards.front: What is the circular increment formula used to update front and rear pointers?
flashcards.back:
index = (index + 1) % capacity

This modulo operation wraps the pointer from the last index back to 0, enabling circular behavior.

flashcards.front: What is the condition to check if a circular queue is empty?
flashcards.back:
front == -1 && rear == -1

Both pointers are set to -1 in the initial empty state and reset to -1 after removing the last element.

flashcards.front: What is the condition to check if a circular queue is full (one-slot-sacrifice method)?
flashcards.back:
(rear + 1) % capacity == front

This checks if the next position after rear equals front. One slot remains unused to distinguish full from empty states.

flashcards.front: Why must circular queues sacrifice one slot instead of using all N slots?
flashcards.back: Without the sacrificed slot, both full and empty states would have front == rear, causing ambiguity. The extra slot resolves this problem.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of enqueue and dequeue operations in a circular queue?
flashcards.back: Both enqueue and dequeue are O(1) — constant time operations. No element shifting is required, unlike linear queues where dequeue is O(n).

flashcards.front: During enqueue of the first element, what happens to front and rear pointers?
flashcards.back: When the first element is enqueued into an empty queue, both front and rear are set to 0 (not -1). This is a special case in the algorithm.

flashcards.front: What are three major applications of circular queues?
flashcards.back:
1) CPU Scheduling (Round Robin algorithm)
2) Buffering data streams (disk I/O, network traffic)
3) Printer spooling and traffic signal systems
